The disease can present with a variety of symptoms related to hormone excess or mass effect. Diagnosis involves imaging studies@ hormonal evaluation@ and pathological examination. Treatment typically includes surgery@ chemotherapy@ and targeted therapies.\""
column34: "\"Symptoms may vary depending on the type and amount of hormones produced by the tumor. Common symptoms include weight gain@ muscle weakness@ high blood sugar@ high blood pressure@ skin changes@ and increased hair growth. In some cases@ tumors may not produce any hormones and are only discovered when they become large enough to cause pain or pressure.\""
column35: "\"The exact causes of ACC are not fully understood@ but genetic mutations and certain inherited syndromes can increase the risk. These include Li-Fraumeni syndrome@ Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome@ and multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1). In some cases@ ACC may be associated with exposure to certain environmental toxins.\""
column41: \Surgery is the primary treatment for ACC. If the tumor can be completely removed@ the prognosis is generally better. Chemotherapy is often used after surgery to kill any remaining cancer cells. Radiation therapy may be used to shrink tumors or relieve symptoms. Targeted therapies are also being developed to target specific molecules involved in cancer growth.\""
column45: \Regular checkups with a doctor can help detect ACC early. People with a family history of ACC or certain inherited syndromes should consider genetic counseling. Avoiding exposure to certain environmental toxins may also help reduce the risk.\""

column49: \Inherited genetic syndromes@ such as Li-Fraumeni syndrome and Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome@ increase the risk of ACC. Exposure to certain environmental toxins may also play a role. People with a family history of ACC are also at increased risk.\""
column50: \ACC can occur at any age@ but it is most common in children under 5 and adults in their 40s and 50s. Some inherited syndromes may increase the risk of developing ACC at a younger age.\""
column51: \Mutations in genes such as TP53@ CTNNB1@ and PRKAR1A have been linked to ACC. These mutations can affect cell growth@ division@ and hormone production. Genetic testing can help identify people at risk of developing ACC.\""
column52: \A family history of ACC increases the risk of developing the disease. This is likely due to inherited genetic mutations that increase susceptibility to the disease.\""
column53: \ACC can be a very aggressive cancer@ but the severity of the disease depends on several factors@ including the stage of the cancer@ the type of hormones it produces@ and the overall health of the patient. Early detection and treatment can improve the prognosis.\""
column54: \Diagnosis of ACC involves a combination of imaging studies@ hormonal evaluation@ and pathological examination. Imaging studies@ such as CT scans and MRI scans@ can help identify tumors in the adrenal gland. Hormonal evaluation can measure the levels of hormones produced by the tumor. Pathological examination of tumor tissue can confirm the diagnosis and determine the type of cancer.\""
column55: \ACC can lead to a variety of complications@ including hormone imbalances@ high blood pressure@ high blood sugar@ and increased risk of infection. In advanced cases@ ACC can spread to other parts of the body@ such as the liver@ lungs@ and bones.\""
column56: \ACC is a rare cancer@ affecting approximately 1 in 1 million people per year. It is slightly more common in women than in men. The incidence of ACC varies by age and ethnicity.\""
column57: \The prognosis for ACC depends on several factors@ including the stage of the cancer@ the type of hormones it produces@ and the overall health of the patient. column35: Adrenal cortex carcinoma is a very rare cancer that occurs when cells in the adrenal cortex develop changes (mutations) in their DNA. A cell's DNA contains instructions that tell the cell what to do. Mutations tell the cells to grow uncontrollably and to continue living when normal cells would die. The accumulating cells form a tumor. The cancer cells can break away and spread (metastasize) to other parts of the body.

column26: Adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD) is caused by mutations in the ABCD1 gene@ which provides instructions for making a protein called adrenoleukodystrophy protein (ALDP). ALDP is located in peroxisomes@ which are compartments within cells that process and break down certain fats and other molecules. Mutations in the ABCD1 gene disrupt the function of ALDP@ leading to a buildup of very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) in cells@ particularly in the brain@ spinal cord@ and adrenal glands. This buildup of VLCFAs damages the myelin sheath@ which is the protective covering around nerve cells. Damage to the myelin sheath disrupts the transmission of nerve impulses@ leading to neurological problems and adrenal insufficiency.

column5: The protein encoded by this gene is a member of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter superfamily. ABC proteins transport various molecules across extra- and intra-cellular membranes. ABC proteins are divided into seven distinct subfamilies: ABC1, MDR/TAP, MRP, ALD, OABP, GCN20, and White. This protein is a member of the ALD subfamily. It is a peroxisomal membrane protein involved in the transport of very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) into the peroxisome for degradation. Mutations in this gene cause adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD), an X-linked recessive disorder characterized by the accumulation of VLCFAs in brain, spinal cord, adrenal cortex, and plasma.
